## Built Factories Pattern

At its core, a factory only needs a URL. In TypeScript we can rely on `import.meta.url` as the starting point for any operation.

<details>

<summary>Outline</summary>

- Sections:
  - Build
  - Options
  - Imported Sources
  - Strong Typing
  - Centralized Configuration
  - Aligned with Next.js App Router
  - Essential Implementation Notes
    - Server / Client Boundary Constraint

</details>

## Props Context Layering

**Purpose**: Enable isomorphic components to work seamlessly with React Server Components while maintaining a single, ergonomic API.

<details>

<summary>Outline</summary>

- Sections:
  - Server-Client Boundary Constraints
  - 1\. Early rendering with fallback values
  - 2\. Conditional async operations
  - 3\. Props-first, context-enhanced hooks
  - 4\. Lazy-load heavy functions
  - Implementation Checklist
  - Real-World Usage

</details>

## HAST (Hypertext Abstract Syntax Tree)

HAST is the core data structure used throughout this package for syntax highlighting and markdown processing. Understanding HAST helps you work effectively with the docs infra.

<details>

<summary>Outline</summary>

- Sections:
  - What is HAST?
  - Why HAST?
    - Build-time Optimization
    - Zero Runtime Overhead
    - Framework Agnostic
    - Customizable Rendering
    - Rich Plugin Ecosystem
    - Efficient Caching
  - Data Flow
  - Structure
    - Example
    - Security Considerations
  - Key Functions
    - Creating HAST
    - Converting HAST
    - Transforming HAST
    - Enhancing HAST
  - Usage in This Package
    - Code Highlighting
    - Type Documentation
  - Performance Benefits
    - Build Time
    - Runtime
    - Comparison
  - Related Specifications
  - Best Practices
    - When to Use HAST
    - Runtime Enhancement Examples
    - Working with HAST

</details>

## Prop Compression

**Purpose**: Reduce page weight and improve hydration performance when sending complex data structures from the server to the client.

<details>

<summary>Outline</summary>

- Sections:
  - The Problem
  - How It Works
    - 1\. Send Plain Text in Initial HTML
    - 2\. Serialize Minimal Props for Hydration
    - 3\. Defer Enhanced Data as Compressed Props
    - 4\. Highlight on Init (Optional)
  - Size Comparison
    - Small Snippet
    - Medium Snippet
    - Very Large Snippet
    - Streaming Comparison
  - Measured Impact
    - Core Web Vitals (Base UI Demos)
    - Page Weight & Performance (Type Documentation)
      - Base UI Combobox
      - docs-infra CodeHighlighter
  - When Not to Use This Pattern
  - Implementation Details
    - Content-Aware Dictionary
      - Checksum Verification
      - Server → Client Data Flow
      - Compact Fallback Format
    - Decompression & Rendering
    - How Compressed Data Arrives
    - Abstracting Complexity from Users
  - Real-World Usage

</details>
